Q1: I tried to organize my study routine around my extracurricular activities, which took up a decent chunk of my time. Usually I'd set aside blocks in the afternoon and evening for coursework, aiming for about four hours on most days. I'm a visual learner, so I spent a lot of time creating diagrams and color-coded notes. Some weeks were more structured than others, honestly, depending on how I was feeling emotionally.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied quite a bit. When deadlines were approaching, I'd cram in more hours and stay up later than usual. During quieter weeks, I tried to keep a more regular schedule, but if I was feeling down or sad, it was harder to stick to any routine. I'd say I was reactive more than proactive most of the time.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about an upcoming deadline or wanting to check if there was new content posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if anyone had responded to my discussion posts. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more often because I wanted to see my progress and earn those badges or points.
Q4: Yeah, around week three or four, checking the LMS became more habitual for me. The gamified elements helped with that because there was always something to check on or a small goal to hit. I'd usually log in after breakfast and again in the evening. It wasn't perfect, but it became part of my daily rhythm for a while.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the visual content. I watched over a hundred minutes of video content because that format just clicks with my brain. The discussion boards were also helpful because interacting with peers made me feel less isolated when I was having sad days.
Q6: I tended to ignore some of the longer reading materials and PDFs. If something wasn't visual or interactive, I'd put it off or skim through it quickly. Some of the optional supplementary readings felt overwhelming when I was already struggling to keep up with the main assignments.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate progress or when it connected clearly to what I'd be tested on. The gamified tasks were great because completing them gave me a sense of accomplishment even on tough days. If something felt abstract or disconnected from my goals, I'd lose interest quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. When I was on top of things, I'd explore them and actually enjoy the extra content. But when deadlines piled up or I was feeling low emotionally, the optional stuff was the first to go. Required activities had urgency that optional ones just didn't have.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of lecture slides and diagrams to study offline on my tablet. I'd also make handwritten visual notes and mind maps that weren't captured anywhere in the LMS. Sometimes I used YouTube videos to supplement concepts that weren't clicking from the course materials alone.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd spend hours reviewing my printed notes and hand-drawn diagrams, but none of that showed up in any log. I'd also study with a couple of classmates in person, quizzing each other and discussing concepts. That peer learning was valuable but completely invisible to the system.
Q11: Yes, quite often actually. I'd download the videos or slides once, then work with them offline for days. My internet is decent but I preferred having materials saved locally so I could study without distractions. The LMS would only show that initial download, not the hours I spent with those materials afterward.
Q12: There were a few weeks when I felt really sad and unmotivated, and it became much harder to engage. My attendance dropped during those periods and I fell behind on some assignments. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars also pulled my attention away at critical moments.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or confused by difficult material, I'd avoid the LMS because it felt overwhelming. Competing responsibilities from my activities outside class meant I had to make tough choices about where to spend my energy. Sometimes I'd log in but not really do anything productive because I was too tired.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. When I was feeling down, I'd go through the motions without really absorbing anything. It looked like engagement on the surface, but internally I wasn't present.
Q15: I postponed some of the harder assignments because they felt intimidating, especially when my confidence was low. Even knowing they were important, the anxiety made me avoid them until the last minute. It's something I'm working on, but it definitely affected my completion rate this semester.
Q16: Deadlines were my main motivator, honestly. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active on the LMS. My engagement was definitely spiked around due dates rather than spread evenly throughout the course. The gamified tasks helped a bit by creating mini-deadlines that kept me more consistent.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback boosted my mood and made me want to engage more. Critical feedback sometimes made me hesitant to continue, especially if I was already feeling sad. I tried to use it constructively, but emotionally it wasn't always easy.
Q18: The course structure with its gamified elements actually helped me stay more engaged than I might have otherwise. Having clear milestones and visible progress made the workload feel more manageable. However, when modules were dense or unclear, I'd fall behind and struggle to catch up.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier because they broke things into smaller, achievable goals. The visual elements in the course materials also helped my learning style. What made it harder was when there was too much text-heavy content or when instructions were ambiguous.
Q20: I think students might be exploring, trying to understand what's expected, or maybe they're struggling with the actual work. High activity could also mean someone is anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. Sometimes you're active because you're lost, not because you're productive.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I spent a lot of time studying offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. Someone could look at my LMS activity and think I wasn't engaged, but I was actually learning through methods that just weren't tracked. The log doesn't capture everything.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns, deadline-driven behavior, and which resources get accessed. But they miss the quality of engagement, emotional state, and all the offline learning that happens. They can't tell if someone is genuinely understanding material or just clicking through.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively trying to understand and connect with the material. It's not just about completing tasks but actually caring about what I'm learning. For me, it also means participating in discussions and feeling like part of a learning community, even when I'm struggling emotionally.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally some weeks, or how the gamified tasks actually helped lift my mood. It wouldn't capture my offline study sessions, my visual note-taking, or the peer study groups. Most importantly, it can't show that despite feeling sad often, I kept pushing forward and passed the course.
